Instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents for further evaluation of the Audit and to ensure that all of the documentary requirements are complete to ensure transparency and propriety of the procurement transactions.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking supporting documents for further evaluation of the Audit Team and to ensure that all documentary requirements are complied with to ensure legitimacy and propriety of the transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team, certified copies of the lacking supporting documents for further evaluation of the transactions; and
Require the Municipal Assessor to cause the registration and transfer of title of the land in the name of the Municipality to ensure ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipal Government.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to require all collecting officers to remit their collections to the Treasurer’s Office within the prescribed considering the distance of the place of their assignment from the Treasury Office.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all Accountable Forms are issued in numerical sequence by performing the procedures mentioned in Section 30 of MNGAS to intensify the control measures over the issuance of the accountable forms and ensure that all collections are accounted, remitted and duly recorded in the books.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Local Finance Committee to avoid the reversion of continuing appropriations unless obligations therefor have been fully paid or otherwise settled and to prepare the Supplemental Budget in accordance with the requirements of Section 321 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that Management:
Require the MPDC, with the assistance of the Municipal Engineer, to plan well and ensure that all PPAs under the 20% DF are procurement-and-implementation ready for an efficient execution thereof; and
Instruct the Municipal Engineer, with the assistance of the MPDC, to formulate a plan and time frame in the implementation of PPAs and to prepare a monitoring report thereof and to require the Municipal Engineer to faithfully supervise the implementation of projects including conduct of inspection of works to ensure timely completion of projects and delivery of its benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to constituents in support to the constitutional objective of providing desired quality of life for all.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure the collection of delinquent real property taxes by requiring the Municipal Treasurer to apply the remedies provided in Sections 254, 256, 257, 258 and 260 of RA No. 7160 and to make information drive campaigns such as posting the notice of the dates when the tax may be paid with discounts and without interest at a conspicuous and publicly accessible place at the Municipal Hall to encourage taxpayers to pay real property taxes in advance or on time in the ensuing years;
Impose interest on the unpaid stall rentals in accordance with the provisions of Section 169 of RA No. 7160 and other remedies consistent with the terms and conditions of the lease contract in case of unjustified failure of compliance by the stall owners; and
Include the payment of delinquent accounts for stall rentals in the policies and procedures of the LGU before approving the renewal of Business Permits and Licenses of stall owners to intensify the collection efforts on delinquent stall rentals.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to confirm periodically the outstanding balances of the borrowers/debtors by sending statement of accounts and demand letters to increase the collections on loans receivable and to instruct the MSWDO to include the requirement of co-makers who will guarantee the re-payment of the loan and penalty provision such as imposition of interest in case the debtor failed to pay on time in the contract agreement and to require the borrowers to settle their loan obligations first before granting requests for renewal.
We further recommended that Management require the MSWDO and the Municipal Accountant to obtain the records on the loans granted prior to year 2002 to enforce collection thereof from the borrowers/debtors.

We recommended that Management require the designated MENRO and the SWM Board to observe the implementation strategies, perform the monitoring programs and prepare and submit the required reports as provided in Chapter 8 of the 10 Year SWM Plan to ensure the implementation of the projects and activities geared towards achieving high waste diversion rate and developing a sustainable solid waste management practice in the locality.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Person, with the assistance of the MPDC, to spearhead the preparation of adequate and systematic GAD Database which will aid in the identification of gender issues and full implementation of the PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget in order to attain the main intentions in promoting gender-responsive governance, protection and fulfilment of women’s human rights and the promotion of women’s economic empowerment.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the OIC HRMO to prepare and transmit promptly the required forms to the GSIS to update the personal records of employees both in the LGU and GSIS to ensure full remittance of premiums to the GSIS.
We further recommended that Management, through the HRMO, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, inform in writing the Head of the GSIS of the discrepancies on the personnel records in the LGU and GSIS database to come up with corrective measures which will enable the remittance of premiums in full to the GSIS and also secure the social security benefits of Municipal employees.

AAR for CY 2019, Page 97-98 | Submitted to the GSIS the required forms in updating personnel records.

Coordinated with other offices in the LGU and GSIS as regard the updating of records.
observations.reason: The monthly personal share of employees and the contribution for employer’s share were not remitted in full amount due to the discrepancies between the records of the personnel of the LGU and the GSIS database, thus reiterated in Observation No. 33, Page 113 of the report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management see to it that the programs and activities incorporated Gender and Development (GAD) Plan of the agency have well-defined gender related issues and aligned with the establishment of the fund in compliance with DBM-NEDA-NCRFW JC No. 2004-1 dated April 5, 2004 to fully address the main intentions in promoting gender-responsive governance, protection and fulfillment of women’s human rights and the promotion of women’s economic empowerment.
observations.managementAction: AAR for CY 2019, Page 99-101 | Regulated the charging of financial assistance such as medical, funeral, medical and educational assistance to the GAD fund.
observations.reason: The gender issues were not well-defined on the eight PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2020, thus reiterated in Observation No. 31, Page 109 of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the BAC and the Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents in conformity with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and ensure strict compliance with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in their procurement transactions.

It was further recommended that Management cause the immediate registration of ownership of the purchased modified rescue vehicle in the name of the Municipality to establish absolute ownership and to protect the interest of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: AAR for CY 2019, Page 102-103 | Instructed the BAC to strictly observe the procurement provisions set by 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.reason:
There were deficiencies noted in the procurement transactions of the LGU as discussed in Observation Nos. 19 and 20, Pages 81 and 85, respectively.

In an interview with the Municipal Treasurer, the ownership of the rescue vehicle was not yet transferred in the name of the LGU. 

No copy of registration of the vehicle in the name of the LGU was provided to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (LDRRMO) to prepare and submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF in accordance with the provisions of Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 to provide a monitoring tool on the financial capability of the LGU to carry out pre-disaster preparedness programs and post-disaster activities and to facilitate the transfer of the unexpended balance of the Quick Response Fund to the special trust fund account as of year-end.
observations.managementAction: AAR for CY 2019, Page 107-108 | Required the LDRRMO and Municipal Accountant to submit the report within the prescribed period.
observations.reason: The Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF were not prepared and submitted within the prescribed period, thus the Municipality was not able to transfer the unexpended balance of the Quick Response Fund (QRF) amounting to ₱884,691.53 and Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (DRRMF) – MOOE amounting to ₱2,313,908.92 or a total of ₱3,198,600.45 for CY 2020 to the Special Trust Fund. Reiterated in Observation No. 32, Page 111 of the report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that management submit immediately the required documents to adhere with COA laws, rules and regulations in order to protect the best interest of the government.
observations.reason: Coordinated with the previous Audit Team for the turnover of the documents concerned, if any.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to request from all depository banks to furnish the Municipal Accountant of complete copies of Credit Memo and Debit Memo every month; and to require the Municipal Accountant to immediately record the reconciling items duly supported with documents in the books of accounts.
We also recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to process the replacement of stale checks consistent with the provisions of Section 104, Volume I of GAM for LGUs.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to recognize the uncollected waterworks system fees in the amount of ₱7,160,024.06 in the books of accounts as receivables and income in order to present fairly the balance of the Accounts Receivable and Waterworks system fees in the financial statements and to prepare subsidiary ledger for each concessionaire.
The following is the suggested journal entry:
Accounts Receivable			 7,160,024.06
Waterworks system fees			   7,160,024.06
We further recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds under the General Fund to cover the water utility expenses of the LGU and the Municipal Treasurer to draw check as payment thereof.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card and to furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year the list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the current year. Based on the list, require the Municipal Accountant to draw a JV to record Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and the corresponding income accounts in order to present the accurate balance of the said receivable accounts in the financial statements.
We further recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to update and reconcile the balance of the delinquent real property tax with the accounts of the taxpayers in the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card of accounts.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accountant to review the transactions recorded in the Due to Other Funds and Due from Other Funds to determine the accounting errors which caused the unreconciled difference of ₱148,601.57 and to prepare the necessary journal entry  using the appropriate accounts prescribed in Section 5, Chapter III, Volume III of GAM for LGUs in order to present the accurate balance of the reciprocal accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accountant to retrieve immediately the supporting documents of the dormant receivable accounts in order to substantiate its balances and to facilitate collection thereof by notifying the accountable officers concerned through the issuance of demand letters. If efforts exerted yield no encouraging results, Management, through the Municipal Accountant, may request for write-off consistent with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant, with the assistance of the Municipal Engineer, to continue to retrieve the records of the road networks acquired prior to CY 2016 and to prepare a summary report thereof in order to facilitate the recognition of all road networks prior to CY 2016 in the books of accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.
We also recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry to recognize the replacement costs of the three road networks implemented in CYs 2014 and 2015 and to provide the necessary depreciation costs to reflect the correct balance of the Road Network, Government Equity, Prior Period Adjustment, Depreciation -  Infrastructure Assets and Accumulated Depreciation accounts in the financial statements.
The following are the suggested journal entries:
Road Networks									  4,273,772.83
Government Equity											 4,273,772.83
To recognize the replacement costs of the road networks acquired on CY 2014 and CY 2015.
Prior Period Adjustments									  1,905,438.88
Depreciation – Infrastructure Asset								 476,359.72
Accumulated Depreciation – Road Network							 2,381,798.60	                                    To provide depreciation costs for five years.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify the costs of completed projects in the amount of ₱14,022,673.46 to the appropriate asset accounts and to provide depreciation costs to the depreciable assets to present fairly the balances of the CIP – Infrastructure Assets, Road Networks, Land, Other Land Improvements, Accumulated Depreciation and Depreciation Expense accounts in the financial statements.
The following is the suggested adjusting entries:
Road Networks						 6,241,842.53
Land									 4,985,695.54
Other Land Improvements				 2,795,135.39							Prior Period Adjustments                          1,843,750.42
Depreciation – Infrastructure Assets           364,530.92
Depreciation – Land Improvements            203,648.12
CIP – Infrastructure Assets                                               14,022,673.46
Accumulated Depreciation – Road Networks                    1,287,052.78
Accumulated Depreciation – Other Land Improvements 1,124,876.68

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to obtain the details of the PPE costing ₱47,895,261.26 and to provide depreciation costs in the books of accounts consistent with the provisions of Paragraphs 59 and 66 of IPSAS 17 and with the policies and procedures provided in Section 262 of GAM in order to present the accurate balance of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to maintain the subsidiary ledger cards for each group of PPE and the subsidiary records for roads and road component for every road network and for the Municipal Treasurer to maintain the equivalent property cards for PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and the Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components which are necessary to support the accuracy and reliability of the balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.
We also recommended that Management require the Head of the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all its PPE and to prepare the inventory report to facilitate the reconciliation of the result of the inventory count with property and accounting records maintained by the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant, respectively, consistent with the guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accountant to obtain the supporting documents/records and details of the financial liabilities totaling ₱2,159,956.78 in order to provide its adequate disclosures or relevant information in the Notes to the Financial Statements and also to notify the payees concerned of the existence of the obligation.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry to reclassify the payments for meals and snacks amounting to  ₱3,297,025.00 to the Foods Supplies Expense account and to correct the entry made in recording the payment for the fabrication of 100 units retaining blocks costing ₱473,649.43 in order to present the correct balances of the Other Maintenance and Operating Expense, Repairs and Maintenance, Food Supplies Expense and CIP-Infrastructure Assets accounts in the financial statements.
The following are the suggested journal entries:
Food Supplies Expense				     3,297,025.00
CIP - Infrastructure Assets 					    473,649.43
Other Maintenance and Operating Expense 			 3,297,025.00
Repairs and Maintenance
– Other Infrastructure Assets                                           473,649.43
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe the description of accounts in Section 5, Chapter III, Volume III of GAM for LGUs in recording future financial transactions for proper presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to monitor the remittance of collections of all collecting officers and the sequential issuance of the ORs by verifying the RCD, checking the statement of accountable forms as to initial balances on hand, receipts, issuance and the ending balances on hand, making physical count of the accountable forms remaining in the custody of the collectors and comparing the same against the ending balance on hand before affixing her signature at the back of the triplicate copy of the last official receipt issued. We also recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to count the money turned over to her before signing the certification and receipt portion in the RCD to provide adequate safeguard of the government funds against possible loss or misuse.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to assign the collection functions to employees occupying permanent positions in order to provide adequate safeguard of government funds against the risk of loss or misuse. We further recommended that Management require the OIC HRMO to prioritize the JO Workers who had shown outstanding performance in their assigned works in hiring additional personnel for permanent positions to perform collection functions.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer with the assistance of the Municipal Assessor to cause the immediate retrieval of all necessary documents of ownership of the parcels of land owned by the LGU and to require the Municipal Assessor to submit application for transfer and registration of ownership with the Land Registration Authority to establish absolute ownership in accordance with Section 39 of PD No. 1445 and Section 449, Volume I of GAAM and protect the interest of the LGU. We further recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds which shall cover the expenses on the registration of parcels of land.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MSWDO and Assistant Municipal Treasurer, to submit the copy of valid ID and other supporting documents and authorization letter of the beneficiaries provided in the schedule for further evaluation of the Audit Team; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant, with the assistance of the MSWDO, to issue demand letters to the 78 recipients of financial subsidy who were not listed in the Social Amelioration Program First Tranche Beneficiaries and to the three beneficiaries who received financial assistance twice to facilitate the refund of financial assistance totaling ₱445,500.00 and return the amounts collected to the DSWD.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that the funds received from the Bayanihan Grant are utilized for COVID-19 related PPAs and to review carefully and determine the conformity of the PPAs with the provisions of the DBM Circular before certifying the availability of allotment in the Bayanihan Grant;
Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to include the warranty clause in the Contract and Purchase Orders for Infrastructure Projects and Goods to be procured using the Bayanihan Grant and to require the submission of the mandatory requirements from the Suppliers prior to the award of contract in accordance with Item 3.5 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 to protect the financial interest of the LGU; and
Require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer (MSWDO) and Municipal Health Officer (MHO), to submit the list of affected families and proof of receipt of distribution of rice, medicines and other relief goods and other supporting documents.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that the funds from the LDRMMF and MDF realigned for COVID-19 related PPAs are utilized in accordance with its intended purpose by requiring the Municipal Budget Officer to review carefully and determine the conformity of the PPAs with the provisions of the DBM Circulars before certifying the availability of allotment;
Require the BAC to include the warranty clause in the Contract and Purchase Orders for Infrastructure Projects and Goods to be procured using the LDRRMF and MDF and to require the submission of the mandatory requirements from the Suppliers prior to the award of contract in accordance with Item 3.5 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 to protect the financial interest of the LGU; and
Require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MSWDO and MHO, to submit the list of affected families and proof of receipt of distribution of rice, medicines and other relief goods and other supporting documents.

We recommended that Management:
Require the BAC to confirm the availability of location and the possession of the lot by the LGU or the project beneficiaries where future infrastructure projects will be constructed by verifying with the records of the Municipal Assessor or by requiring the project beneficiaries to submit proof of ownership to avoid delays in the implementation of the projects;
Instruct the BAC to verify, inspect and test the required capacities and operating conditions of equipment offered by the winning bidder and to check the performance of the bidder on its on-going or completed projects and incorporate the results thereof in the Post Qualification Report in order ensure that government contracts are awarded to qualified bidders; and
Require the BAC, through the BAC Secretariat, to post the NOA, Contract and NTP in the agency website, to verify the correctness of the amount of ABC and number of contract duration of the infrastructure project in the ITB, to invite the required observers on time and to require the bidder to submit the OSS and Bid Form in prescribed format and attend in the opening of bids to enhance the transparency of the procurement transactions.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Engineer to require the contractors concerned to cause the immediate repair of the cracks and scaling in the three road network projects to avoid penetration of the water and prevent further deterioration that may affect the efficiency and effectiveness of the project.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC to require the suppliers to submit the mandatory documents in the use of alternative method of Small Value Procurement to ensure that the most advantageous price, to the LGU are obtained from suppliers of known qualifications and to instruct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all of the documentary requirements are complete to ensure transparency and propriety of the procurement transactions.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the MPDC to make an inquiry with the PRA about the issuance of the Notice of Full Compliance and NTP or permit for the reclamation project and to comply with the requirements of the said national agency to facilitate its approval and to submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management require the disbursing officer to furnish the Audit Team with the payroll duly signed by the senior citizens and to submit the photocopy of the OSCA ID, claim stubs from DSWD and the Warranty and Release from Liability of the concerned SocPen beneficiaries to support the propriety of transactions.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that cash advances for payroll are duly supported with approved payroll or list of payees indicating the net amount for the pay period.
Further, we recommended that the disbursing officer ensure that the payrolls are duly signed by payees and are supported with DTR, accomplishment reports and authority from the claimant and identification documents, if claimed by person other than the payee to support the propriety of transactions.

We recommended that Management ensure strict adherence to the provisions regarding the disbursements of SEF by requiring the Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with the Local School Board Members, to utilize the SEF within the allowable expenses only and to avoid disbursement of funds for the programs provided with separate funds by the Department of Education in order to maximize the purpose of the fund.

We recommended that Management ensure the optimal utilization of the 20% of the IRA by requiring the MPDC to formulate a plan and time frame in the implementation of PPAs and to prepare a monitoring report thereof and to require the Municipal Engineer to faithfully supervise the implementation of projects including conduct of inspection of works to ensure timely completion of projects and delivery of its benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to constituents in support to the constitutional objective of providing desired quality of life for all.

We recommended that Management ensure the collection of delinquent real property taxes by requiring the Municipal Treasurer to apply the remedies provided in Sections 254, 256, 257, 258 and 260 of RA No. 7160 and to make information drive campaigns such as posting the notice of the dates when the tax may be paid with discounts and without interest at a conspicuous and publicly accessible place at the Municipal Hall to encourage taxpayers to pay real property taxes in advance or on time in the ensuing years.
We also recommended that Management impose interest on the unpaid stall rentals in accordance with the provisions of Section 169 of RA No. 7160 and to require the Municipal Treasurer to include the amount of unpaid stall rentals and interests in the next issuance of demand letters to the delinquent stall owners. We further recommended that Management include the payment of delinquent accounts for stall rentals in the policies and procedures of the LGU before approving the renewal of Business Permits and Licenses of stall owners to intensify the collection efforts on delinquent stall rentals.

We recommended that Management instruct the MSWDO to include the requirement of co-makers who will guarantee the re-payment of the loan and penalty provision such as imposition of interest in case the debtor failed to pay on time in the contract agreement and to instruct the Municipal Accountant to confirm periodically the outstanding balances of the borrowers/debtors by sending statement of accounts and demand letters to increase the collections on loans receivable.
We further recommended that Management require the MSWDO and the Municipal Accountant to obtain the records on the loans granted prior to year 2002 in the amount of ₱320,600.82 to enforce collection thereof from the borrowers/debtors.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to identify the specific gender issues caused by the unequal situation between women and men in the locality in the GAD Plan and Budget and to formulate PPAs that are responsive to these gender issues in order to attain the main intentions in promoting gender-responsive governance, protection and fulfilment of women’s human rights and the promotion of women’s economic empowerment.
We further recommended that Management, through the GFPS, prioritize in the succeeding GAD Plan and Budget the recurring/emerging gender issues in the Municipality, results of gender assessment of the organization or its PPAs and other GAD PPAs identified in the GAD Code of the LGU that have not been considered in the previous year to achieve a more effective GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF at the end of each month in order to determine immediately the unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRMF – MOOE and to facilitate the transfer to the Special Trust Fund account in order to present the balances of the current year unspent DRRMF in the Financial Statements as of year-end.

We recommended that Management ensure full remittance of premiums to the GSIS by requiring the OIC HRMO to prepare and transmit promptly the required forms to the GSIS to update the personal records of employees both in the LGU and GSIS.
We further recommended that Management, through the HRMO in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, inform in writing the Head of the GSIS of the discrepancies on the personnel records in the LGU and GSIS database to come up with corrective measures which will enable the remittance of premiums in full to the GSIS and also secure the social security benefits of Municipal employees.

We recommended that Management insure the Waterworks facilities, Municipal Tourism Building and Hospital and Health Centers and other properties relevant in the delivery of public service to provide safeguard against substantial losses in the event of damage to, or loss of, properties brought by fortuitous events by requiring the Municipal Treasurer with the assistance of the Municipal Accountant to identify the insurable assets and to submit the inventory report to the GSIS and Audit Team consistent with the provision of  COA Circular No. 2018 - 002 dated May 31, 2018. We also recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds needed for the payment of premiums.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure adherence to the provision of Section 16 of RA No. 9003, otherwise known as “Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000" by causing the immediate completion and submission of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan which shall serve as the foundation, guide and timetable in the identification, implementation and operation of solid waste management programs; and requiring the concerned officials and employees to strictly observe the criteria provided in Sections 41 and 42 of RA 9003 in the establishment and operation of Sanitary Landfill to ensure the protection of public health and its environment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the employees concerned to provide the cost segregation and description for road components of road projects in the inventory report for Local Road Networks of the LGU in order to facilitate the recognition of the Local Road Networks prior to Calendar Year 2016 in the books of accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 and to present fairly the balance of the Road Network account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the immediate retrieval of all necessary documents of ownership of the unregistered lots owned by the Municipality to facilitate the transfer and registration of ownership with the Land Registration Authority in accordance with Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume I of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual to establish absolute ownership and protect the interest of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure adherence with the provisions of Section 20 of Manual on the New Government Accounting System by addressing the concern on inadequacy of manpower and by requiring  the Municipal Treasurer to maintain the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card and duly certified list of taxpayers which shall serve as the basis of the Municipal Accountant in setting-up the receivables for real property and special education taxes at the beginning of the year and to facilitate the reconciliation of accounts for fair presentation of the RPT and SET receivables in the financial statements.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive ensure adherence to the provision of Section 111(2) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 by directing the Municipal Accountant to analyze the affected transactions and prepare the corresponding adjustments consistent with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed by COA Circular 2015-009 pursuant to Section 113 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 in order to present the accurate balance of the reciprocal accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive cause the immediate retrieval of all necessary documents of the dormant accounts and cause collection of the amounts due by notifying the accountable officers concerned through the issuance of demand letters. Otherwise, file for a request for write-off pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure compliance with the provisions of Section 111 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 in keeping financial records by requiring the Accounting Unit to exert efforts in obtaining the supporting documents/records and details of the financial liabilities concerned in order to provide its adequate disclosures or relevant information in the financial statements; and to notify the payees concerned of the existence of the obligation once efforts yield encouraging results.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure compliance with the requirements of Section 63(D) of the Local Treasury Operations Manual by reconciling the daily balance of the Cashbook – Cash in Treasury and Cashbook – Cash Advances with the cash on hand to avoid incurrence of negative or abnormal balances of Cash in Vault account at the end of the month or cut-off date as the case may be.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require all collecting officers to remit their collections to the Treasurer’s Office within the prescribed period in compliance with Section 29, Volume 1 of the NGAS Manual and issue official receipts in strict numerical sequence to provide adequate safeguard of the government funds against possible loss or misuse. It is further recommended that the collecting officers concerned submit written explanation why delays in remittance of collections are incurred even if the place of their assignment is just nearby the Office of the Treasury.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive ensure compliance with the provisions of Sections 3.2 and 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 by instructing the Municipal Treasurer to request from all depository banks complete copies of Credit Memo and Debit Memo every month and to furnish the Municipal Accountant copies of the same; and to require the Municipal Accountant to immediately record all valid reconciling items that require adjustment and correction in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the Cash in Bank account in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict compliance with the procurement provisions of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (RA) No. 9184 and the fundamental principle on financial transactions prescribed in Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445; and to instruct the BAC and the Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents prescribed in conformity with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the return of the unexpended balances of fund transfers after the completion of the projects to the Source Agency in compliance with Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 and Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to enable the National Government to use the fund for other purposes beneficial to the public.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Ensure strict compliance with the procurement provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 (R.A. 9184) and the fundamental principle on financial transactions prescribed in Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that disbursements are supported with complete documentation in conformity with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445; and
Determine the actual date of delivery of the Brand New Ambulance and impose the penalty or liquidated damages equivalent to one-tenth (1/10) of once percent for every day of delay incurred by Toyota Bicutan, Parañaque from December 25, 2018 pursuant to the terms and conditions of the approved Purchase Order.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict compliance with the procurement provisions of the Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 (R.A. 9184) and the fundamental principle on financial transactions prescribed in Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445; and to instruct the officials and employees concerned to submit the required documents prescribed in conformity with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive engage the services of consultants only when the relevant experience, level of expertise or attention required for the government project or related activities is beyond the optimum in-house capability of the LGU in compliance with the provisions of Annex “B”, General Principles of Consulting Services, of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) and COA Circular No. 2012-003, to avoid incurrence of unnecessary expenditures.
It was also recommended that the required documents be submitted by the officials and employees concerned pursuant to Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 for further evaluation of the Audit Team.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain a checklist of supporting documents as required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to ensure the completeness of the supporting documents before certifying in the Disbursement Voucher.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Ensure strict adherence with the revised guidelines on the implementation of Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation (AICS) prescribed by DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 11 s. 2019 to avoid excessive payment of financial assistance; and
Direct the MSWD Officer to provide adequate control mechanisms such as maintenance of ledger per beneficiary which will contain his pertinent information and the date and type of assistance availed of.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict adherence to the provisions of Section 22 of the Omnibus Rules on Leave, Rule XVI of the Implementing Omnibus Rules Book V of EO 292 in granting monetization of leave credits to avoid excess payments and disallowance in audit. We further recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly recognize in the books the said payments for monetization of leave credits consistent with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed by COA Circular 2015-009 pursuant to Section 113 of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure adherence to the provisions of Section 4, paragraph 6 of Presidential Decree (PD) 1445 and Local Budget Circular No. 103 by requiring the officials and employees concerned to submit the required documents to support the claims for Representation and Transportation Allowances; and instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to see to it that the claims for RATA are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer exert utmost effort to submit the accounts and financial reports within the prescribed period for timely examination and verification of accounts and financial report in compliance with Section 7.2.1 of the 2009 of the Rules and Regulations on the Settlement of Accounts, Section 39 (1) and (3) of P.D 1445, and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure adherence to the provisions of Section 74 of P.D. 1445, Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-001 dated October 2, 1996 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code by instructing the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the Bank Reconciliation Statements for the month of May 2019 and November 2019 together with the corresponding bank statements and to observe the prescribed period of submission to ensure timely verification and reconciliation of the balances of Cash in Bank accounts of the Municipality with the depository bank and for fair presentation of balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive strictly comply with the requirements prescribed in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001, and to cause the submission of copies of contracts to the Audit Team within five working days from its perfection in order to allow the Audit Team to perform effective review and generate timely and relevant audit results.
Further, take appropriate administrative disciplinary action as provided in Section 4 of the same Circular against the responsible officer concerned for unjustified non-compliance with the Circular.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to prepare and submit to the Head of the Procuring Entity the Procurement and Monitoring Report and submit the same to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 9184 and GPPB Circular 03-2015 dated March 20, 2015.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure adherence to the provision of Section 16 of Republic Act 9003, otherwise known as “Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000" by causing the immediate completion and submission of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan which shall serve as the foundation, guide and timetable in the identification, implementation and operation of solid waste management programs; and requiring the concerned officials and employees to strictly observe the criteria provided in Sections 41 and 42 of RA 9003 in the establishment and operation of Sanitary Landfill to ensure the protection of public health and its environment.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure compliance with the provisions of Section 7 of the Rules and Regulation in the Settlement of Accounts; and cause the settlement of Notice of Disallowances that has already become final and executory and for which the corresponding COA Order of Execution has been issued. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure optimal utilization of the fund transfers from the National Government agencies in accordance with the existing laws, rules and regulations by requiring the officials and employees concerned to faithfully supervise and monitor the implementation of the projects stated above and all other projects funded by the National Government Agencies within the timeframe in order to deliver the desired benefits of the programs, projects and activities on time to the constituents of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure the optimal utilization of the 20% of the IRA in accordance with the provisions of Section 287 of the Local Government Code and Section 5.0 of the DILG and DBM Joint Circular Memorandum No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 by formulating a plan and time frame in the advertisement of their procurements for infrastructure project which will ensure the completion of the projects on target dates and delivery of the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents of the Municipality towards the attainment of the constitutional objective of providing desired quality of life for all.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure timely remittance of premiums in adherence to Section 6(b) of Republic Act No. 8291, otherwise known as GSIS Act of 1997, by requiring the Human Resource and Management Office to transmit promptly to the GSIS the required forms in updating the records of the personnel; and in coordination with other offices concerned, to update the personnel records of employees both in the LGU and GSIS.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management see to it that the programs and activities incorporated Gender and Development (GAD) Plan of the agency have well-defined gender related issues and aligned with the establishment of the fund in compliance with DBM-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-1 dated April 5, 2004 to fully address the main intentions in promoting gender-responsive governance, protection and fulfillment of women’s human rights and the promotion of women’s economic empowerment.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive require the municipal officials concerned to:
Conduct the required annual physical count of the PPE as of December 31 of every year. The actual count may be undertaken any month during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end. This procedure is a must in order to know the physical existence of the Property Plant and Equipment and their conditions, and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets.  Reconciliation of the result of the count with the accounting records is to be undertaken and effect the necessary adjustments, if any, and to pin point responsibility/accountability for missing PPE’s;
Maintain/update the Subsidiary Ledgers, PPE Ledger Cards, Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs), and put Property Tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring of the PPE’s and facilitate  identification  thereof: and
Control and monitor in the use of Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for semi-     expendable items to avoid loss as these are usually small items.
Furthermore, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive appoint a General Services Officer/Supply Officer to take custody of and be accountable for all properties owned by the local government unit, to perform all other functions pertaining to supply and management heretofore performed by the local government treasurer, and to enforce policies on records creation, maintenance and disposal.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count of road networks and record in the books of accounts its equivalent amount and disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements pursuant to Items VI (2) and IX (3) of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management enforce the liquidation by accountable officials of their cash advances for local travels within the reglementary period. We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert more effort in tracing the difference and make the necessary adjustments to present accurate balances of the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds in the financial statements.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that management should see to it that Gender and Development (GAD) Plan of the agency will fully address and adhere to the basic purpose and objectives towards the role of women in becoming a participative partner in nation building and development, with the end view of protecting their lives and rights and to live a modest life in a society.

observations.recommendation:
For the proper management and handling of the PPE, we reiterated the following
recommendations:
 Conduct the required complete annual physical count of the PPE as of
December of every year. The actual count may be undertaken any month
during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end. This procedure is
a must in order to know the presence/physical existence of the fixed assets and
their conditions; and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets
to come up with the RPCPPE and to be submitted to Auditor concerned not
later than January 31 of each year. Reconciliation of the result of the count
with the accounting records is to be undertaken to effect the necessary
adjustments; and to pin point responsibility/accountability/ liability for
missing PPEs.
 Maintain/update the subsidiary ledgers, PPE cards, AREs and placing of
property tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and
monitoring of the PPEs and to facilitate in identifying them.
 For control and monitoring make use of ICS for semi-expendable items to
avoid loss, as these are usually small items.
 Instruct the designated General Services Officer (GSO) or Supply Officer to
prepare/maintain stock cards, property cards per category of property, plant
and equipment, as well as in the conduct of physical count and the
preparation of RPCPPE for appropriate property & supply management.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry voucher to effect the necessary
adjustments to correct the misclassified accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant exert
more effort in reconciling the Intra-agency accounts, Due to Other Funds and
Due from Other Funds to promptly identify any discrepancy in the balances of
the two accounts and to be able to effect the necessary adjustments, if
appropriate, to bring the affected accounts into agreement.

We recommended that management take the following courses of action:
 Strictly adhere with the pertinent provisions of RA 10121, COA Circular
No. 2012-002, DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-001 in the management, accounting and recording of Calamity Fund;
 Require the Municipal Accountant to:
 Prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to cause the transfer of the
unutilized amount of DRRMF-QRF and MOOE after five years
covering CY 2011-2012 not reverted back to the General Fund as
required under Section 5.1.13 of the abovementioned circular;
 Maintain separate registry of appropriations, allotments and
obligations (RAAO) for QRF, MOOE and CO;
 Maintain subsidiary ledgers for transfers of agency’s unutilized
DRRMF to the Special Trust Fund by year of transfer; and
 Stop the practice of preparing journal entries to window dress the
balance of account Trust Liability–DRRM account (2-04-01-020)
under Trust Fund book in contrary with the established accounting
rules and practices.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant cause the immediate
adjustment of valid reconciling items to present the correct balances of the Cash
in Bank and other affected accounts. Moreover, make a written notice to the
owner of the stale check for its proper disposition/replacement in accordance
with Section 189 of GAAM, Volume I.

We reiterated our recommendation to the management the following
courses of action:
 Submit the complete documentary requirements for the conduct of
auditorial review of contract and for the payment of claims in
compliance with COA Memo Circular No. 2005-027 and COA Circular
No. 2012-001.
 Ensure that 10% retention money is being deducted from the progress
payment to the contractor to serve as warranty for hidden defects
during the construction period and shall only be released upon final
acceptance of the project and/or if the contractor request for the
substitution of the retention money for each progress billing; and
1%retention money deducted from the total contract price for the
payment of delivered goods and shall only be released after the lapse of
the warranty period.
 Ensure the submission of all the perfected contracts together with the
complete supporting documents in accordance with the relevant
provisions of COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to enable
the auditor to conduct a systematic and effective review process with a
view of generating timely and relevant audit results.
 Require the BAC Secretariat to attach a checklist on all perfected
contracts as guide to those employees checking the documents before
submission to the Office of the Auditor. Likewise, for the Municipal
Accountant to also attach checklist on disbursement vouchers and see to
it that complete supporting documents were present before payments
are made.

We recommended that management explain the acceptance of the projects
as 100% complete when in fact there were defects in the work and/or deviation
from plans and specifications. In addition, require the contractor to complete the
deficiencies noted and if warranted, refund the value of all the work items
involved in the contract not accomplished and/or faulty.

We recommended that management instruct the concerned officials to
strictly follow the pertinent provisions of GPPB Circular No. 8-2015 in the
preparation of the Annual Procurement Plan for proper planning of all
procurement activities and for sound fiscal management. Likewise, PPMP
prepared should be revised to support the approved APP mentioned in Sections
7.3.2, 7.3.3, 7.3.4 and 7.3.5, Rule II of the revised IRR of RA 9184 be fully
complied, for easy validation of items procured against the approved plan.

We reiterated our recommendation that management submit complete
supporting documents for the legality and validity of transactions.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
 Require all Accountable Officers to liquidate their cash advances on time,
otherwise, impose administrative sanctions against them for any unjustifiable
reasons due to failure to liquidate cash advances within the prescribe period,
in accordance with Section 89 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 dated
February 10, 1997 and Civil Service Commission Memorandum Circular No.
12, series 2012.
 Cause or order the withholding of any money claims against the accountable
officers concerned until they have fully settled their unliquidated cash
advances.
 Request for the write-off of dormant outstanding cash advances if warranted
in accordance with COA Resolution No. 80-122 and COA Circular No. 2016-
005.
 Instill to all LGU officials and employees the necessity of submitting
complete and proper documentation for all claims on travel pursuant to the
provisions of COA Circular No. 96-004 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
 For lodging expenses that exceeded the authorized amount, submit the
necessary certifications from the LCE declaring these as absolutely necessary
in the performance of duties.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management accomplish a Statement of Itemized
Peace and Order Program presenting the specific activities to ensure effective
and efficient control of the Confidential Fund, and to further corroborate the
propriety of the allocated amount for the said fund.

We reiterated our recommendation that management ensure the
responsible officials and employees, as withholding agents, should see to it that
GSIS premium contributions withheld are accordingly remitted on or before
their due dates to avoid the possible additional interest imposed by IRR of RA
8291 for the non-remittance of the same and also the probability that the
benefits of employees may be affected, if non-remittance will continuously
occurred.

We reiterated our recommendation that management maximize the full
implementation of all development projects/programed from the 20%
Development Fund for the year it has been appropriated to give its constituents
the timely benefits that will be derived therefrom.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Accounting Office in
cooperation with the Treasury Office to ensure the complete submission of
accounts within the prescribed period for timely examination and verification of
accounts pursuant to Section 7.2.1 of the 2009 of the Rules and Regulations on
the Settlement of Accounts, Section 39 (1) and (3) of P.D 1445, thus to avoid
disciplinary actions and/or penalties under Section 23 (1) and (3) of The
Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.

We reiterated our recommendation that management submit immediately
the required documents to adhere with COA laws, rules and regulations in order
to protect the best interest of the government.

We recommended that management discontinue the practice of referring
brand names in its procurement activities.

We reiterated our recommendation that management instruct the
Municipal Accountant as well as the Municipal Treasurer to remit in full and
without delay the RPT shares of the province as required under Section 272 of
RA 7160 in order that programs/projects/activities of the province could be well
implemented.

We reiterated our recommendation that management formulate programs,
projects and activities that are gender related to conform with the purpose of
establishing the GAD Plan, that is, to address gender issues and women
empowerment.

We recommended that management comply with the mandatory
requirement of providing 1% of IRA for the local councils for the protection of
children for strengthening and implementation of its programs, projects and
activities as required under DILG Memorandum No. 2012-120 and Section 15 of
RA 9344.

We reiterated our recommendation that management take the following
courses of action:
 Strictly observe the provisions in the Municipal Ordinance for the
collections of delinquent accounts on the rentals and rights on
properties owned by the Municipality.
 Require the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to apply more
measures in the collection of delinquent accounts and to include in the
back accounts the surcharge of twenty-five (25%) percent on the total
rent due.
 Enforce legal sanctions provided in Section 5B.03 of the Municipal
Ordinance, if warranted, which states that “failure to pay the rental
fee for two (2) consecutive months shall cause automatic cancellation of
the contract of lease of stall, without prejudice to suing the lessee for the
unpaid rents at the expense of the lessee and the stall shall be declared
vacant and subject to adjudication.”
 Assign a full-time Market Supervisor, who is directly assign or
designated to this job, to handle the proper management of market
operation.

We reiterated our recommendation that management require the
suppliers/contractors on the issuance of Sales Invoice and/or Official Receipts on
various procurement of goods and services made in compliance with Revenue
Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014 dated January 13, 2014 and likewise, strictly
observed said regulations on future disbursements made.

 Conduct the required complete annual physical count of the PPE as of December of every year. The actual count may be undertaken any month during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end. This procedure is a must in order to know the presence/physical existence of the fixed assets and their conditions; and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets to come up with the RPCPPE and to be submitted to Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year. Reconciliation of the result of the count with the accounting records is to be undertaken to effect the necessary adjustments; and to pin point responsibility/accountability/ liability for missing PPEs.
 Maintain/update the subsidiary ledgers, PPE cards, AREs and placing of property tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring of the PPEs and to facilitate in identifying them.
 For control and monitoring make use of ICS for semi-expendable items to avoid loss, as these are usually small items.
 Instruct the designated General Services Officer (GSO) or Supply Officer to prepare/maintain stock cards, property cards per category of property, plant and equipment, as well as in the conduct of physical count and the preparation of RPCPPE for appropriate property & supply management.

observations.recommendation:
For the proper management and handling of the PPE, we have reiterated the following recommendations:
Conduct the required complete annual physical count of the PPE as of December of every year.  The actual count may be undertaken any month during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end.  This procedure is a must in order to know the presence/physical existence of the fixed assets and their conditions; and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets to come up with the RPCPPE and to be submitted to Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year.  Reconciliation of the result of the count with the accounting records is to be undertaken to effect the necessary adjustments; and to pin point responsibility/accountability/ liability for missing PPEs.
Maintain/update the subsidiary ledgers, PPE cards, AREs and placing of property tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring of the PPEs and facility in identifying them.
For control and monitoring make use of ICS for semi-expendable items to avoid loss, as these are usually small items.
Most importantly, designate or create an item for General Services Officer (GSO) or Supply Officer that will handle/maintain stock cards, property cards per category of property, plant and equipment, as well as in the conduct of physical count and the preparation of RPCPPE for appropriate property & supply management.
We have reiterated our recommendation that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for depreciation to reflect a more accurate valuation of various PPE accounts.
We have reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry voucher to effect the necessary adjustments to correct the misclassified accounts.

We have reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant exert more effort in reconciling the Intra-agency accounts, Due to Other Funds and Due from Other Funds to promptly identify any discrepancy in the balances of the two accounts and to be able to effect the necessary adjustments if appropriate to bring the affected accounts into agreement.

We have reiterated this audit finding to call the attention of the Municipal Accountant due to continues inaction on the reconciliation of loans payable account to bring into agreement the balances of accounting records with the bank, if necessary, effect immediately the corresponding adjustment.

We have recommended that management take the following courses of action:
Explain the deviation of procurement classification from infrastructure project to goods; likewise, submit bidding documents for infrastructure transactions required under Section 9.1.1 of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and some of the provisions of RA 9184.
Submit the complete documentary requirements for the conduct of auditorial review of contract and the payment of claims in compliance with COA Memo Circular No. 2005-027, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and other pertinent provisions of the revised IRR of RA 9184.

We have recommended that management take the following courses of action:
Submit the complete documentary requirements for the conduct of auditorial review of contract and for the payment of claims in compliance with COA Memo Circular No. 2005-027 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
Ensure that 10% retention money is being deducted from the progress payment to the contractor to serve as warranty for hidden defects during the construction period and/or delivery of goods and shall only be released upon final acceptance of the project.
Ensure the submission of all the perfected contracts together with the complete supporting documents in accordance with the relevant provisions of COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to enable the auditor to conduct a systematic and effective review process with a view of generating timely and relevant audit results.
Require the BAC Secretariat to attach a checklist on all perfected contracts as guide to those employees collating the documents before submission to the Office of the Auditor.  Likewise, for the Municipal Accountant to also attach checklist on disbursement vouchers and see to it that complete supporting documents were present before payments are made.

We have recommended that management instruct the concerned officials to strictly follow the provision of item 3.2 of GPPB Circular No. 1-2009 in the preparation of the Annual Procurement Plan for proper planning of all procurement activities and for sound fiscal management.  Likewise, PPMP prepared should be revised to support the approved APP mentioned in Sections 7.3.2, 7.3.3, 7.3.4 and 7.3.5, Rule II of the revised IRR of RA 9184 be fully complied, for easy validation of items procured against the approved plan.

We have recommended that management explain the incurrence of expenditures in excess of what was collected.  Failure to offer a valid justification will result to the disallowance of the transaction for being not in conformity with the pertinent provisions of DBM Budget Circulars.  Likewise, submit complete supporting documents for the legality and validity of payment and lastly, draw a journal entry voucher to effect the misclassified entries made for proper presentation in the financial statements.

We have recommended that management require the Accountable Officer to deposit her collections promptly and intact as prescribed under COA Memorandum No. 2013-004 for the efficient discharge of her duties and avoid the risk of possible loss or misuse of funds. Moreover, require the Revenue Collecting Personnel to remit his collections at the close of each business day pursuant to the provisions of Section 29, Vol. I,  of the NGAS Manual.
We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere with the guidelines prescribed in the Manual on New Government Accounting System for the preparation and verification of Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms. Likewise, ensure to submit the report to the Auditor on or before the 5th day of the ensuing month.

We have reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Require all Accountable Officers to liquidate their cash advances on time, otherwise, impose administrative sanctions against them for any unjustifiable reasons due to failure to liquidate cash advances within the prescribe period, in accordance with Section 89 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Civil Service Commission Memorandum Circular No. 12, series 2012.
Strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 and 97-004 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
Cause or order the withholding of any money claims against the accountable officers concerned until they have fully settled their unliquidated cash advances.
Request for the write-off of dormant outstanding cash advances if warranted in accordance with COA Resolution No. 80-122.
Submit to the auditor the noted missing documents and cause the refund of the noted excess in claimed registration fee.

We have reiterated our recommendation that management ensure the responsible officials and employees, as withholding agents, should see to it that GSIS premium contributions withheld are accordingly remitted on or before their due dates to avoid the possible additional interest imposed by IRR of RA 8291 for the non-remittance of GSIS withheld within the due dates and also the probability that the benefits of employees may be affected, if non-remittance will continuously occurred.

We have recommended that management cause the full implementation of all projects programed from the 20% Development Fund for the year it has been appropriated to give its constituents the timely benefits that will be derived therefrom.
We have recommended that management explain the incurrence of expenditures without an approved budget or appropriation.  Failure to offer a valid justification will result to the disallowance of the transaction for being not in conformity with the pertinent provisions of RA 9184 and RA 7160.

We have recommended that management submit immediately the required documents to adhere with COA laws, rules and regulations in order to protect the best interest of the government.
We have recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant as well as the Municipal Treasurer to remit in full and without delay the RPT shares of the province as required under Section 272 of RA 7160 in order that programs/projects/activities of the province could be immediately implemented.

We have recommended that the Accounting Office in cooperation with the Treasury Office ensure the complete submission of accounts within the prescribed period for timely examination and verification of accounts pursuant to Section 7.2.1 of the 2009 of the Rules and Regulations on the Settlement of Accounts,   Section 39 (1) and (3) of P.D 1445, thus to avoid disciplinary actions and/or penalties  provided for under Section 23 (1) and (3) of The Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.

We have reiterated our recommendation that management formulate programs, projects and activities that are gender related to conform with the purpose of establishing the GAD Plan, that is, to address gender issues and women empowerment.
We have recommended that management strictly observe the rules in the conduct of Small Value Procurement under R.A. 9184 and to provide justification in incurring the foregoing hotel accommodation expenses with consideration to economy, prudence, suitability and accessibility of the venues.

We have recommended that management take the following courses of action:
Strictly observe the provisions in the Municipal Ordinance for the collections of delinquent accounts on the rentals of personal and real properties owned by the Municipality.
Require the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to apply more measures in the collection of delinquent accounts and to include in the back accounts the surcharge of twenty-five (25%) percent on the total rent due.
Enforce the sanctions provided in Section 5B.03 of the Municipal Ordinance, if warranted, which states that “failure to pay the rental fee for two (2) consecutive months shall cause automatic cancellation of the contract of lease of stall, without prejudice to suing the lessee for the unpaid rents at the expense of the lessee and the stall shall be declared vacant and subject to adjudication.”
Assign a full-time Market Supervisor to handle the proper management of market operation.

We have recommended that management before a project is implemented, proper planning, study and assessment should be made to see to it that its goal and objective on the proposed project will be achieved efficiently and effectively.

We have recommended that management require the suppliers/contractors on the issuance of Sales Invoice and/or Official Receipts on various procurement of goods made in compliance with Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014 dated January 13, 2014 and likewise, strictly observed said regulations on future disbursements made.

We recommended that management require all concerned officials and employees to apply the appropriate withholding tax rates on all government purchases in accordance with BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 to prevent revenue loss and to avoid possible penalties arising from non-compliance thereof.

a. We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer/General Services Officer as well as the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain complete reports/ records like the Supplies Ledger Cards, Stock Cards and Property Cards for the proper monitoring of properties purchased, donated, transferred or disposed of so that reconciliation of accounting records with the physical count will be completed. Likewise, it is recommended that copy of RPCPPE be submitted within the deadline as set forth in the pertinent provisions of MNGAS.

b. We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry voucher to effect the necessary adjustments to correct the misclassified accounts.

c. We have recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for depreciation to reflect a more accurate valuation of various PPE accounts.

d. We have recommended that the Accounting Office in coordination with the Rural Health Unit secure the complete submission of documentary requirements to support the receipt of the donated medical equipment so as to warrant the authenticity and validity of said transaction and its ownership by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management:

•	Strictly observe the provisions in the Municipal Ordinance for the collections of delinquent accounts on the rentals of personal and real properties owned by the Municipality.

•	Require the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to apply more measures in the collection of delinquent accounts and to include in the back accounts the surcharge of twenty-five (25%) percent on the total rent due.

•	Enforce the sanctions provided in Section 5B.03 of the Municipal Ordinance, if warranted, which states that “failure to pay the rental fee for two (2) consecutive months shall cause automatic cancellation of the contract of lease of stall, without prejudice to suing the lessee for the unpaid rents at the expense of the lessee and the stall shall be declared vacant and subject to adjudication.”

•	Assign a full-time Market Supervisor to handle the proper management of market operation.
